title: Experience Kayaking at Travlr Getaways
author: Juan De La Cruz
excerpt: Whether you're a first-time paddler or a seasoned water sports enthusiast, kayaking at Travlr Getaways is an experience like no other.
body: Whether you're a first-time paddler or a seasoned water sports enthusiast, kayaking at Travlr Getaways is an experience like no other. Glide across calm lagoons, navigate mangrove tunnels teeming with wildlife, and discover hidden coves only accessible from the water. Our certified guides lead small-group tours at sunrise and sunset for the most breathtaking views.

All equipment is provided, including sit-on-top kayaks, paddles, and personal flotation devices. No prior experience is needed. Our guides will have you confidently on the water within minutes. Tours run twice daily and can be booked at the resort activities desk or through our website.

After your paddle, cool off with a complimentary tropical smoothie at the beachside bar and relive the highlights from your waterproof action camera footage, which our team captures throughout the tour.

Tour groups are intentionally small — we cap each session at six paddlers per guide — so there is always a leader within arm's reach if you have a question or need a quick tip on form. That ratio also means your group sets the pace, not a crowd of strangers. Families with kids as young as eight, solo adventurers, and groups of friends all feel equally at home.

If you want to extend the trip, we offer a half-day mangrove-and-cove combo that pairs kayaking with a stop at a secluded snorkeling reef. Lunch is brought out to the cove by boat — fresh ceviche, grilled fish, and chilled drinks served on a floating platform. It is one of those experiences that ends up in everyone's highlight reel.

title: Top 10 Diving Spots Around the World in 2026
author: Marina Patel
excerpt: From the Great Barrier Reef to the cenotes of Mexico, we've ranked the world's most breathtaking underwater destinations for divers of every skill level.
body: Picking ten diving spots out of the entire planet is a thankless job, but our editors put in the work. The 2026 list reflects shifts in coral health, visibility data from the past two seasons, and reader-submitted rankings from over four thousand certified divers.

Top honors this year go to Raja Ampat in Indonesia for sheer biodiversity — there is nowhere else on earth where you can see four hundred coral species and a thousand reef fish in a single week. The Yucatan cenotes take second place for their otherworldly fresh-water cathedrals, while Sipadan, Palau, the Red Sea at Dahab, the Great Barrier Reef's Ribbon Reefs, the Galapagos, the Maldives, Bonaire, and Cocos Island round out the top ten.

If you are new to diving, start with Bonaire or the Maldives. Both offer shallow house reefs you can step into from shore, calm currents, and Open Water certification programs that take just three days. For experienced divers, Cocos Island and the Galapagos are the bucket-list adventures — strong currents, big animals, and unforgettable encounters with hammerhead schools.

Travlr Getaways will be expanding its diving package lineup later this year. Reef trips currently include Gale Reef, Dawson's Reef, and Claire's Reef, with new departures to Bonaire and Raja Ampat in the planning stage.

title: 2026 Best Beaches Contest: The Winners Are In
author: Liam Carter
excerpt: Thousands of travelers voted and the results are finally here. See which beaches claimed the top spots for clearest water, whitest sand, and best overall experience.
body: Over twelve thousand travelers cast votes in our annual Best Beaches Contest. The 2026 winners span four continents, and a few will surprise you.

Whitehaven Beach in the Whitsundays takes home the top prize for whitest sand, beating out Hyams Beach by just under two hundred votes. The silica content there really does feel like flour underfoot. For clearest water, the win went to Anse Source d'Argent in the Seychelles — a destination we feature in our Seychelles Paradise Cruise package.

The Best Overall category went to Navagio Beach in Greece. Surrounded by limestone cliffs and only accessible by boat, Navagio combines drama with that classic Mediterranean blue water. Honorable mentions: Pink Sands in the Bahamas, Tulum's beaches in Mexico, El Nido in the Philippines, and Reynisfjara in Iceland for sheer striking contrast.

What surprised us most in the data: travelers increasingly weight sustainability and crowd density over pure aesthetics. Beaches with active reef-protection programs and reservation caps consistently outscored equally beautiful but overrun alternatives.

title: New Fishing Ban to Protect Endangered Coral Reefs
author: Dr. Elena Voss
excerpt: International conservation groups have announced a new protected zone covering over 200 square miles of reef ecosystem.
body: A coalition of three regional governments and four conservation NGOs announced this week that a new no-take fishing zone will be enforced across roughly 215 square miles of reef ecosystem starting in June 2026. The protected zone covers some of the most biologically dense reef habitat in the Caribbean basin.

For travelers, the change is largely good news. Diving and snorkeling will continue in regulated zones, and the recovery of fish populations is expected to dramatically improve sightings within two to three years based on the recovery curves seen in similar protected areas. Spearfishing tours and recreational hook-and-line angling will be prohibited within the boundary.

Tour operators inside the zone have been working with conservation groups for nearly a year to prepare. Existing dive boats are required to switch to mooring buoys (no anchoring on coral), and dive guides have completed a mandatory reef-impact training program.

If you are booking a reef trip in the next twelve months, ask your operator whether they have the new conservation certification. Travlr Getaways has confirmed that every reef package on our catalog (Gale, Dawson's, and Claire's) uses certified operators.

title: Travlr Lifeguard Saves Child in Dramatic Beach Rescue
author: Aisha Kim
excerpt: A quick-thinking Travlr Getaways lifeguard prevented a tragedy at one of our partner resorts last week.
body: Last Saturday afternoon at one of our partner resorts on the Yucatan coast, a six-year-old boy was caught in a sudden rip current about thirty meters offshore. The water had been calm all morning, and the family had not realized that an offshore wind was building.

Lifeguard Andres Solis spotted the boy within twelve seconds of the trouble starting, signaled the second tower, and reached the boy with a rescue buoy in under a minute. The child was visibly shaken but unharmed, and was reunited with his family on the beach.

Andres has been a certified open-water lifeguard for seven years and joined the Travlr partner network in 2023. "It is what we are trained for," he said when asked about the rescue. "The key is reading the water before anyone is in trouble."

Every Travlr partner resort employs at least two certified lifeguards on duty during designated swim hours, and all family packages include a complimentary beach safety briefing on the first morning. We also provide age-appropriate snorkel vests and quick-release rip-current ID bracelets for kids on request.

title: Beginner's Complete Diving Checklist: What to Pack
author: Marina Patel
excerpt: Heading on your first dive trip? We've put together the ultimate packing checklist (essential gear, travel documents, in-water accessories) so you don't miss a thing.
body: Most resorts and dive operators provide the bulky equipment — tanks, BCDs, regulators, weight belts — but there are a handful of things you should always bring yourself. Comfort matters underwater, and the gear closest to your body is the gear most worth owning.

Bring your own mask. A leaky rental mask can ruin an otherwise perfect dive, and a properly fitted mask costs about forty dollars and lasts years. Same logic for snorkels and fins — these are inexpensive personal items that drastically improve the experience.

Pack a dive logbook (or use a digital app), a basic dive computer if you have one, a swim cap or hair tie for long hair, defog drops, a microfiber towel, and reef-safe sunscreen — the kind without oxybenzone or octinoxate. Many destinations now legally require reef-safe sun protection.

Documents matter too. Bring your certification card (or digital copy), a recent diver medical clearance if it has been more than a year since your last one, and proof of travel insurance with dive coverage. Trip-level coverage often excludes scuba, so a specialty dive insurance rider is wise.

Finally, do not underestimate the importance of dry bags. A small ten-liter dry bag is cheap and saves your camera, phone, and dry clothes from the boat ride. Travlr Getaways includes a complimentary dry bag in every diving package booked through 2026.

title: 7 Must-Try Local Dishes on Your Next Tropical Trip
author: Sebastien Laurent
excerpt: Great travel is as much about food as it is scenery. Our in-destination food guides highlight the authentic dishes you simply can't miss.
body: Some destinations are remembered for their views, but the very best are remembered for what you ate. Here are seven dishes worth planning a meal around.

First, ceviche on the Pacific coast — anywhere from Mexico down to Peru. The freshest white fish, lime, salt, and onion, served within minutes of preparation. Order it at a small beachside place where the fish was on the boat that morning.

Second, satay in Bali or Malaysia. Skewered, grilled meat with peanut sauce sounds simple, and that is the point — the technique is everything, and the smoky char from a coconut-husk fire is what makes it sing.
Third, fresh poke in Hawaii. Forget the chain-restaurant version — find a small shop that makes it daily, and order the ahi shoyu or the simple sea-salt-and-seaweed style.

Rounding out the list: a proper Greek souvlaki with tzatziki straight from a kalymnos taverna, ropa vieja from a family-run paladar in Havana, fresh injera with three lamb-based stews in Ethiopia, and the unforgettable laksa from a hawker stall in Penang. Each one is worth a flight.

title: Beyond the Beach: Rainy-Day Adventures in Phu Quoc
author: Linh Tran
excerpt: Monsoon season got your beach plans soaked? Vietnam's southern coast has more to offer indoors. From classic shooting galleries to virtual aquariums, here's how a wet afternoon turns into one of the highlights of the trip.
body: Phu Quoc gets roughly three months of monsoon weather between July and September, but rainy days here are nothing to fear. The island has built a surprising number of high-quality indoor attractions, many of them centered around the VinWonders complex on the northern tip.

VinWonders itself is a full-scale theme park — think Universal Studios meets Sentosa — with rollercoasters, a water park (covered sections for monsoon days), an aquarium with a walk-through tunnel, and a beautifully designed Vietnamese culture pavilion that is genuinely educational.

Outside the park, look for the night market in Duong Dong town. Most of the food stalls are covered, and the seafood is sourced from boats that came in that afternoon. It is one of the best food-market experiences in Southeast Asia.

For families, the indoor arcade and the aquarium are usually full-day options for kids. Adults often gravitate to the pearl farms — Phu Quoc's signature export — where you can see how cultured pearls are produced and pick up a souvenir made on-site.
